title: The trouble with digital twins is how quickly they become authoritative
summary: This one looks at digital twins as instruments of authority as much as mirrors of reality.
topic: digital twins once maintenance became the real product
body: There was a version of digital twins circulating in December 2025 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. More often it is a story assembled only after someone has chosen the conditions that make the trend look inevitable. Standing inside a train ride where the future looked mostly like error handling, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

I keep finding the topic interesting because it refuses to line up cleanly with anyone’s preferred camp. Digital twins can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I take the question to be about what kinds of obedience and attention the tool normalizes under the cover of convenience.

In 2025, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were using the systems partly as tools and partly as ways of auditioning who they wanted to be. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some were just tired of tasks that felt repetitive, exhausting, and beneath anyone’s best attention. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. The trouble begins when a feeling gets promoted into an institution, because institutions do not disappear as easily as enthusiasm does.

Over time I have learned to watch what drops out of a story once people begin repeating it for a living. Most of the missing pieces come from the same family: labor, repair, emotional burden, governance, failure handling, and uneven inconvenience. Digital twins looked different to me once I framed it that way. The elegant solution started to look less elegant and more like a pile of trade-offs with excellent styling. That does not make the thing useless. Often the first honest service criticism provides is simple legibility.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It shows me both the movement of the technologies and the movement of my own attention. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Digital twins sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from December 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I no longer find the forecast itself as interesting as the human bargain each tool quietly proposes to its nearest users. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.
